summary:
The pure histological landmark indicating when to stop endodontic treatment involves identifying the apical constriction as it marks a definitive boundary preserving dental pulp tissue integrity without over-preparation risk. Key focus remains strictly aligned around this anatomically defined point crucially marking healthy limits during procedure completion phases.
To summarize Agent1’s reasoning:

The primary histologic marker signaling the conclusion of root canal therapy is the identification of the apical constriction, which delineates safe working lengths while protecting remaining viable dentin-pulp complex from unnecessary removal. This specific anatomic feature provides clear guidance against excessive instrumentation beyond its natural confines. 

This summary captures two core aspects emphasized by Agent1—the importance of recognizing the apical constriction—and focuses directly on what constitutes essential knowledge regarding procedural endpoints based purely upon biological markers.
